speaker: interviewer
text: They're trying to get the light.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little more. A little more. A little more. Just like that. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking Hasidim who were smuggling. I think this is kind of an interesting--

speaker: interviewee
text: But in a funny way, we didn't think it was anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those peasant women, my aunt apparently was very-- she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go ahead. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, her name was-- I mean, first name was Etka-- Etka [NON-ENGLISH]. And my uncle, her husband, he wasn't very much of a businessman. He was sort of sitting in the-- you know, in the Yeshiva and learning. And she was actually the bread-- the bread giver. So everybody knew her, you know, and here came Etka's nieces and Etka's daughters and everybody was giving-- they would say milk a cow and give us the fresh milk, like. Have you ever-- have you ever drank milk directly from a cow? Have you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family--my family had a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, so you do. But I thought it was something absolutely delicious. And they would give us-- as I say, they would give us a kilogram of butter or a dozen eggs. And so we came back and distributed it between our-- my aunt's household and our household. And-- and as I say, maybe for the first two months things were not bad at all. And also, we didn't wear anything-- we didn't have to wear anything. We left already, apparently, when the yellow star was. But when we came to the-- it was Skierniewice, which was part of the [NON-ENGLISH]-- this was also true for Warsaw. At the beginning you didn't wear a yellow star. You wore a white band with a-- with a star of David, with a little star of David. You know that, I suppose. I'm sure that so many things you heard already from other survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we want to her them from you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this took quite some time until we had to wear this. And for the first-- it's funny that we don't even remember-- it must have been maybe for about six months, there was no ghetto in Skierniewice. And that was another thing which was really funny in a way. We had this one-room apartment which my aunt found for us before. And it was sort of cozy. I don't know how big it was. I remember the first Passovers there. It was scrubbed. We all learned how to scrub the floors and the table-- you know the wooden table at home because tables are cheap in Poland because of the Polish, you know, peasant girl would come, and you didn't have to be a rich person to have-- to have a so-called maid, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and it sort of-- and I remember I even felt that it was sort of an adventure in a way. And-- and we lived across the street from the what you call [NON-ENGLISH]? A base-- an army base, the Polish army base which was taken over by the Germans. And every so often, because we lived with those peasants, or those others that they had to come to this-- get whatever they needed from the-- the Germans, some permits for this or for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so off-- and I was out of school and I really spoke German as my foreign language and I spoke it really quite-- quite well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every so often, somebody would-- some Polish peasant would knock at the door and he'd say, do you speak any German? And foolishly, I said would say, yes. And they would, well, can you help me because, you know, he didn't know any German and whoever the German soldier was, they couldn't communicate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a couple of times, even-- but this is, again, another story-- a couple of times one of them, after-- there was-- he came-- he says if I should come to his house, wherever he lived, he would give me a duck-- a live duck. And that was the first time when I was so upset.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way back, there were Polish peasant women standing there in front of this base. I mean, each one of them needed something, or he-- and here I come with this duck. And they started literally attacking me physically-- from whom did you-- I mean, in Polish if they really want to offend you, they call you [POLISH]-- from whom did you steal that duck? They took it away from me, they beat me up. I remember I was so-- that was really the first time and I was trying to be, you know, sort of behave like a lady about it. And I said, aren't you ashamed? I am a Pole. I was born here, I went to school here. I even help when you-- if you come to ask me for some help because I happen to speak German because I finished school already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were so-- so this was really the first time, that-- and then a very short time later there was a-- a ghetto announced. And we had to move from this place, and we moved to a part of the ghetto place. And this what a time when also they started-- but they were preparing the ghetto, it wasn't so fast. They were building bridges-- Ben told you, maybe, he was-- no. The things which they were doing, you know, that was the same thing. Around those Polish towns, you know, the big cities, especially, had those very narrow streets. So there were several streets which were assigned to be ghettos, designated to be ghettos.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on one side was a sidewalk, and the street in the middle-- what you call it here? [LAUGHS] You know, it's funny when you talk about those times I begin to think in Polish, because that's how I was living it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- so on one side, OK-- there was-- those-- there was a sidewalk and the street and the houses, where the-- which were not in the ghetto. The other side of the street, only the houses-- houses, they were little shacks-- belonged to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got out of your little shack, or your little room or whatever, there was a bridge which they built. And you had to-- I mean from the side where-- in order-- but the sidewalk was not allowed for you to be walked on. So you had to take the bridge to go to the side of-- to that side where the sidewalk was allowed. Ben, I thought he said once, because he was out the architectural school, that he was working a little bit with this. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and after all this was done, a short time later-- maybe it was-- I think that it was-- I'm almost sure that it was sort of around New Year's, after Christmas 1940, that they announced that the ghetto in Skierniewice is going to be liquidated because Skierniewice is going to become Judenrein, and you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, but during this period, did-- did you experience witness any sorts of beatings or hangings or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't witness any-- any hangings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beatings or when Jews were called to the central area or synagogue burnings? Can you describe anything you might have-- that the Germans might have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. At that time in Skierniewice I don't think it was taking place. The only thing that-- that, if you happen to find yourself in the street when you were coming across a German-- first of all, the Jew had to take off his hat for-- you know, in front of every German. And if you didn't do it, or if he felt like beating you up, sometimes you would come home bloody. But I-- but in Skierniewice I didn't-- maybe they were taking place, I really wouldn't know. Because one didn't run around the city. I can assure you there wasn't-- so, but I remember it-- and then the winter came, and then things were getting harder and harder just to live. We were not going to anymore to those peasant villages. And I remember there was this one other tenant in our-- I don't know-- it-- it was like a house. There were stairs up, we lived downstairs. There were maybe three or four families that lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man-- and everybody was looking to find some-- something to do to earn some living. So I remember that this was for a very short time. He came to my parents-- and of course, this was-- meant me-- would I go into business with him? He will-- he will go out into the villages and bring wood. You know, I mean the houses were heated by-- by wood or by coal. I mean there was no central heating, especially in the small places. Most of the houses didn't even have any indoor [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] plumbing and water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and-- and that I should-- and I should become this-- say, I should go to some of those so-called families that are well recognized in Skierniewice. One of them was Ben's brother's family, also. And I should go and try to, you know, get the orders for this wood, then we will deliver it. So it was going maybe for about, I don't know, maybe a month. But I was not a very good saleslady, I don't think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it helped a little bit, but it didn't help very much. And then-- So you said the ghetto was liquidated. You got word that they were going to liquidate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The ghetto-- and then, of course, this was that whole big enterprise that was the brainchild of my cousin, who-- he was from Skierniewice. And he had very many, actually very many, Polish, non-Jewish friends. I don't, maybe he drank a little, maybe he played cards a little. But I really don't know-- Shia was his name. But he was very-- unfortunately, he got-- he was also killed in one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Warsaw-- so anyway, the Jews had to leave Skierniewice. They had the-- they could go to Warsaw-- actually, everybody was supposed to go to Warsaw, which was about 60 kilometers. No transportation was being provided. It really became quite a panicky situation. So my cousin, Shia, he was-- [NON-ENGLISH] was the name of this Pole. I remember he's also the one who gave us those little, you know, birth certificates which were totally false.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it started that-- and this was my mother's, there were war conferences, I suppose. There were those rumors that Skierniewice is going to become Junderein, because all those places are on the west side of the Vistula. The Vistula divides north and south Poland. And there were rumors that on the east side of the Vistula, maybe there are not ghettos yet. And since my sister and I began-- started knocking ourselves around, you know, some people from [NON-ENGLISH], which was very close, to Miedzyrec-- came to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: It turned out that they were actually being deported there, because they wanted to make that whole area Judenrein. Did you-- did you read that book, You know the book by Christopher Browning? That's-- that's the region that he writes about. I have a couple of letters from him because Miedzyrec is written up in it, also-- it's the time when I was there. So anyway, that we should go-- my mother had a cousin in a place called Siedlce, which was on the east side of the Vistula-- that we should go to Siedlce.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, traveling by train was absolutely punishable-- everything was punishable by death. By that time, we really were supposed to wear already those-- no, this was- yes, we we're supposed to wear those white bands, the arm bands. So you took off the arm band and you just took your-- but I don't know. I don't even think that you had any feeling of-- I don't know. I suppose when you are young, you are-- it was not any bravery. It was-- I don't know, but we did it several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember we went to-- we went to We found this family. The family's name was Katz. You are making a noise. Bernard, you're making noises. Noises off. Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came there, we found them. I mean, everything went fine. They-- the family's name was Katz. Her name was Leah, she was my mother's first cousin. And they told us yes, there is no ghetto in Siedlce, that is true. But we should definitely not come to Siedlce, because one of those commandants there is one of the worst anti-Semites. He gets up in the morning and he has to get himself two Jews killed before he can have his breakfast. But that's how the Miedzyrzec Podaski came about. But not far from Siedlce, another 40 kilometers, 60 kilometers, there is a city called Mezrich. In Polish it means Miedzyrzec Podaski-- I'd never heard of it before. There's a large Jewish population and the industry there are furs, fur skins, and badgers, badger brushes. I don't even know till now why badger brushes were so important. And this is an industry that the Germans need for the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is no ghetto, the people supposedly still have a very rather peaceful life. Why don't you take the train and go to Miedzyrzec and find out? And we did. Who-- your mother, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my sister and I, we went. We were the shiksa scouts. We came there, and we talked to-- I mean the place looked to me-- I mean there was a good part also, but quite-- but some of the girls with whom I was in Germany were all-- were really from within this one family. We talked to-- we asked, it looked so-- the Jews, most of them, lived in apartments we call [NON-ENGLISH]. I know Shmul is such a Yiddish name like Moishe, Shmul, Yitzhak, or whatever. And--but we talked to a few families, and one of them who was a well-to-do baker, actually, and the Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], he was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they said yes, it is true. Things are-- things are so far, because the Germans need us and everybody is working. And there was this river running, and the [NON-ENGLISH] was along the river. That's where they were washing all those-- I mean the smell was awful. They were washing all those the skins, those animal skins.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back with this information which we got and that's when my cousin-- then came this one episode which stuck in my mind because it was really out-- like out of a movie. My cousin and this [NON-ENGLISH], they decided they are going to make a business out of it, so we would make-- earn some money, the two families-- and of course, this Pole also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was all-- actually, it was all sort of not allowed. I mean, if the Germans appeared-- but I really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- this Pole, he hired a moving company from Warsaw, which were trucks, not horse and buggy trucks, but trucks-- water, ice trucks. And we started to offer it in Miedzyrzec-- who wants to go, because in Warsaw, everybody was so bad and everybody got to be so, you know, terribly concerned what to do, what to do, and where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever wants to go to Miedzyrzec-- for that and that much money-- I don't know how much it was charged, and everything. You-- we would organize it, and we will-- it is-- it's a moving deal. And that's really what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my cousin and was this-- this [NON-ENGLISH], they were communicating with whoever was a representative of this moving company. And then one day, they came and they said that I should take the train to Warsaw and pick up the contract from this man. And they told me his name and said, when I get off the-- well-- the train depot, the main train depot in [NON-ENGLISH], he looks so and so. And they would him how I look, and OK. So you weren't taking the train into the ghetto, the Umschlagplatz, you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no platz yet in Warsaw, see-- there was no ghetto yet in Warsaw. This was 1940. The ghetto was not closed yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I wasn't sure--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't come from Warsaw. I was coming from-- I was coming from Skierniewice, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were going to Warsaw now, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to Warsaw now not as a Jew-- to meet this Pole. I was going to Warsaw as a Pole. And so I came-- it was maybe 5 o'clock in the afternoon, I do not remember anymore. And also, this is another thing. You know-- furs, wearing a fur, was absolutely also punishable by death. This was this action before in Poland. You know, this kind of-- I mean, we were not allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I-- but-- it was winter, and Poland is cold. I think I must have, my mother-- it wasn't even expensive. It's was, what do you call the seal skins? Seal skins? Which was, I think, one of the cheapest furs. So I-- I took my mother's coat, and anyway, here I get off the-- the train and meet this gentleman, and he's very courteous, and very-- I mean, he treat me like-- you know-- I don't know what he-- and he says, well, I think before we do anything with the papers, let's go and have some dinner. And that was the first time I ate a non-Kosher meal. And it was in it a-- it was-- it was sort of like a nightclub. I don't-- what would you call it here? It was a-- there was dancing, there was music. A dinner club.

speaker: interviewee
text: A dinner club, OK. [NON-ENGLISH], it was called. And there I come, you know, and he treats me like his best girlfriend. And we talk about literature a little bit, we talk about this a little bit. And he asked me to dance, and I dance. And he ordered some drinks and I drink. I mean, I just-- you know, when I think of it, me at that time, I was-- I was-- what was I? Not quite 19 or 20 years old. But suddenly I am a Pole among the Poles. He was very gentlemanly. And he showed me all those papers and he signed this-- of course, I had all the instructions from my cousin. You know, what-- and then he takes me back to-- in the evening, takes me-- I mean, to the-- I took the train back and came to Skierniewice, and that's how that thing started. So-- and that's when, since we had, you know, those buses-- buses-- actually, trucks, these were trucks-- going either to Krakow, either to Warsaw. Whoever wanted to go to Warsaw or to Miedzyrzec, whoever wanted to go to Miedzyrzec. And many people still, you know, they had family in Warsaw, and nobody knew how to know how bad it's going-- it's going to be. People were going also to Warsaw. And Bernard's family-- his mother, his brother, and he-- went to Warsaw. And that gave me again-- I think of it-- a chance to travel to Warsaw several times. But another thing was, when I was at the Siedlce-- that man had a business. I don't know. I think yeah, he had-- that's what he did also before-- before the war, house paint.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was-- we're we're talking about, you know, what I'm doing, what we are doing in, what my parents are doing, and how do we live, and that whole thing. So he says, you know, if you could-- of course, you know the Jewish business, he couldn't-- he didn't have anything. But he said, if you could-- I have a few customers, or-- or-- not customers, but people from whom he was buying in the Warsaw ghetto-- if you could smuggle to me some paint, I would pay for it. I would pay you for it. So I went back home to Skierniewice and we-- and, you know, at that time things were-- you were getting poorer and poorer and we were buying [SIGHS] potatoes. I remember once I went with my sister because the boys were always, you know, afraid to go. And have you ever carried on your back 50 kilograms of potatoes? I remember when I was walking with it, I was thinking that probably to have a baby cannot be any more pain than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean-- some of those thoughts come back to me. I remember I was so-- and my sister, poor thing, she was carrying-- and then we distribute. And again, you sold some of it, you know, gave it to your aunt, and distributed it to some neighbors and charged for it, because everything was so precious. And people were afraid to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway so when Bernard's family went to Warsaw, I went to Siedlce, I came to Warsaw, got my-- and that's how maybe two or three times-- this was in March already, 1940-- and brought those-- there's another thing. The wall was already on in Warsaw. I had friends of mine-- their parents lived on [NON-ENGLISH], which was a small ghetto-- I don't know if anybody knows about it-- a small ghetto that was liquidated into the big ghetto. But they lived right at the small ghetto. So I stayed with them. Bernard would come, and I- I would go and get my paint. This was, you know, this was an awful lot of black market going on in the ghetto, too-- fortunately, otherwise people would starve even more. And I bought those two-- I don't know how heavy they are, another 50 pounds maybe. I don't know, you know those big cans of paint, house paint, and went to stay the evening with those friends of mine from [NON-ENGLISH]-- that family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Bernard would come in the evening. Of course, we had the nice visit, and at about midnight-- the train was going back, I think, about 5 or 4 o'clock in the morning. So at midnight I would climb through that walls, and Bernard and another friend of ours, [NON-ENGLISH], would-- on the-- on the cord would lift those paints over the wall and then I would pick it up and go the-- but I did it maybe two or three times, and then Passover was coming. And I went home for the holidays and I got typhus. And I was the first one in the family that got typhus, which is, you know, like fever, which really comes from the-- we were so [NON-ENGLISH]-- I mean, I think back, and I think this was one of the worst scourges. They were just eating you up. And I was the only one who had it. And at that time, it was still being-- I think I was quarantined. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were already in Miedzyrzec. And I remember another thing. When I came home, I was again, this one room, whatever we had in there was, also. And I was-- I was so weak, you know. It was-- people would come in and would say, you know, my face was-- would come in and say-- she looks like a Madonna, like Raphael's Madonna. My face was so-- and really I couldn't even stand on my feet. And I remember-- and this I don't want to tell, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I always cry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother would go out every morning, maybe at 5 o'clock-- I don't know where she went-- and brought me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an orange. I have no idea where she got it and how much she spent for it. Every time I think of it. [CRYING] There are two places that I can't refrain from crying. Well, anyway, that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: She brought you the orange. She would bring you the orange.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would bring these oranges as if she think that this is going to make me well. And we were-- eating a piece of-- a small piece of black bread, if you were fortunate to buy it. There was this Jewish bakery. What was the name? [NON-ENGLISH], I think. We would get-- if they had enough, they would also provide us with some bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to change the tape now, OK? And

speaker: interviewee
text: I wonder, where did she get that orange? I have no idea. At that time an orange was something. Besides, in Poland oranges were not growing like in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
